question: A coworker finds a 45-year-old diabetic man confused and sweating at his desk. His skin is pale cool and clammy. He cannot answer questions appropriately but can follow simple commands. Blood glucose reads 42 mg/dL. What should you do?
option_a: Call 911 and wait for paramedics before doing anything
option_b: Give oral glucose immediately since he is conscious and can follow commands. Blood sugar of 42 with altered mental status is hypoglycemia. Oral glucose can be lifesaving and should not be delayed
option_c: Give him insulin to lower his blood sugar
option_d: Have him drink a cup of coffee to wake up
rationale: Blood glucose of 42 with altered mental status confusion pallor and diaphoresis is hypoglycemia. Since he can follow commands and swallow give oral glucose immediately. Hypoglycemia can cause brain damage if untreated. Do not wait for ALS if you can treat now.

question: After giving oral glucose to the hypoglycemic patient you recheck his blood sugar 10 minutes later. It is now 98 mg/dL and he is alert and oriented. He refuses transport saying he feels fine. What should you advise?
option_a: He is cured and does not need further care
option_b: Advise him that blood sugar can drop again and he should eat a substantial meal with protein and carbohydrates. Encourage transport for evaluation. If he still refuses document refusal thoroughly and advise him to seek medical care
option_c: He needs more oral glucose to be safe
option_d: Nothing his blood sugar is normal now
rationale: While his glucose is now normal it can drop again especially if he has not eaten. Advise eating a substantial meal with protein to maintain glucose. Encourage transport for evaluation of why hypoglycemia occurred. If he refuses document thoroughly including risks explained.

question: You find an unconscious diabetic patient. Blood glucose shows HIGH which indicates over 500 mg/dL. She is breathing rapidly and deeply and has a fruity odor on her breath. What condition is this?
option_a: Hypoglycemia
option_b: Diabetic ketoacidosis DKA. Very high blood sugar fruity breath from ketones and Kussmaul respirations deep rapid breathing attempting to compensate for acidosis indicate DKA. This is a serious emergency requiring IV fluids and insulin at the hospital
option_c: She ate too much sugar
rationale: Very high blood glucose over 500 with fruity breath from ketones and Kussmaul respirations indicates DKA. This develops over hours to days typically in Type 1 diabetics. She needs IV fluids insulin and electrolyte management at the hospital. This is not treatable in the field.

question: A diabetic patient is found unconscious. You cannot get a blood glucose reading and are unsure if it is high or low blood sugar. She cannot swallow safely. What should you do?
option_a: Give oral glucose anyway
option_b: Do not give oral glucose to an unconscious patient who cannot protect their airway. Maintain airway position appropriately provide oxygen and transport rapidly. IV glucose or glucagon will be needed
option_c: Give her insulin just in case
option_d: Wait until she wakes up to give glucose
rationale: Never give anything by mouth to an unconscious patient as they will aspirate. Without the ability to check glucose or give IV medications your role is supportive. Maintain airway provide oxygen and transport for definitive care. Glucagon or IV glucose will be given.

question: A 68-year-old man suddenly developed right-sided weakness and is having difficulty speaking. His wife noticed the symptoms started about 45 minutes ago. Using FAST what findings would you expect?
option_a: Face normal Arm normal Speech normal
option_b: Face drooping on right. Arm drift or weakness when right arm raised. Speech difficulty with slurring or trouble finding words. Time to call 911 and note symptom onset of 45 minutes ago
option_c: Only leg weakness is seen in stroke
option_d: Symptoms would develop gradually over days
rationale: FAST findings in stroke include facial droop arm weakness and speech difficulty. Time of onset is critical as clot-dissolving treatment is only effective within a few hours. Document that symptoms began 45 minutes ago for the receiving hospital.

question: The stroke patient's wife asks if her husband is going to be okay. She wants to know what is happening. How do you explain this while providing care?
option_a: Tell her he is definitely having a stroke and may die
option_b: Explain you are concerned he may be having a stroke where blood flow to part of the brain is reduced. Time is critical because treatments exist that must be given quickly. You are taking him to a hospital that can provide specialized stroke care
option_c: Tell her nothing is wrong to keep her calm
option_d: Explain the brain is permanently damaged
rationale: Provide honest compassionate information. Explain concern for stroke and that time-critical treatments exist. Emphasize the importance of rapid transport to a stroke center. Avoid false reassurance or excessive doom. Family members need information to cope and make decisions.

question: A patient has sudden onset left-sided weakness. When asked to smile the right side of her face moves normally but the left side droops. When she holds both arms out the left arm drifts downward. Her speech is slurred. BP is 188/96. Should you treat her blood pressure?
option_a: Yes immediately lower her blood pressure
option_b: No do not treat blood pressure in the field for stroke. Elevated BP during stroke may be the body's attempt to maintain brain perfusion. Lowering it could worsen brain ischemia. Transport rapidly and let hospital specialists manage BP
option_c: BP does not matter in stroke
rationale: Blood pressure is often elevated during acute stroke as the body tries to maintain cerebral perfusion. Field treatment of hypertension in stroke is generally not recommended as it may worsen ischemia. Rapid transport to a stroke center is the priority.

question: You respond to a call for a 72-year-old woman who woke up with left-sided weakness. She went to bed at 10 PM and woke at 6 AM with symptoms. What is the significance of this timing?
option_a: The stroke occurred at 6 AM when she woke
option_b: The stroke could have occurred anytime during the night. The last known normal time is 10 PM when she went to bed. For treatment decisions the relevant time is last known normal which is 8 hours ago limiting treatment options
option_c: Timing does not matter
option_d: She has been having a stroke for 8 hours
rationale: When stroke symptoms are discovered after sleep the onset time is unknown. The relevant time for treatment decisions is last known normal which is when she went to bed. This limits time-sensitive treatments but she still needs rapid evaluation.

question: You witness a coworker suddenly stiffen and fall to the floor. He then begins rhythmic jerking movements of all extremities. His eyes are rolled back and he is unresponsive. What should you do during the seizure?
option_a: Hold him down to stop the movements
option_b: Protect him from injury by moving objects away but do not restrain him. Do not put anything in his mouth. Note the time the seizure started. Time the duration. Most seizures stop on their own within 1-2 minutes
option_c: Put something in his mouth to prevent tongue swallowing
option_d: Pour cold water on him to stop the seizure
rationale: During a seizure protect from injury by clearing the area but do not restrain the patient. Never put anything in the mouth as this can cause injury. Time the seizure duration. Most generalized seizures stop spontaneously within 2 minutes.

question: The seizure stops after about 90 seconds. The patient is now limp unresponsive and breathing shallowly. What is this phase called and what should you do?
option_a: He is dying you need to start CPR
option_b: This is the postictal phase. Roll him onto his side recovery position to protect his airway. Suction if needed. Monitor breathing. He will gradually become more responsive over minutes. Stay with him and reassure him as he awakens
option_c: The seizure is not really over
rationale: The postictal phase follows a seizure characterized by unresponsiveness then confusion and gradual recovery. Position on side to protect airway from secretions or vomiting. Monitor breathing and provide reassurance as the patient slowly awakens often confused and disoriented.

question: A patient has been seizing continuously for 8 minutes with no signs of stopping. His lips are becoming blue. What is this condition and why is it dangerous?
option_a: This is normal for some seizures
option_b: This is status epilepticus which is a medical emergency. Seizures lasting over 5 minutes rarely stop on their own and cause hypoxia brain damage and can be fatal. This requires immediate ALS intervention with medications and rapid transport
option_c: Long seizures are not dangerous
option_d: He will wake up on his own eventually
rationale: Status epilepticus seizures lasting over 5 minutes is life-threatening. Continuous muscle activity causes hypoxia hyperthermia and brain damage. These seizures require medication to stop. The cyanosis indicates severe hypoxia. This is a true emergency requiring immediate ALS and transport.

question: A mother calls 911 because her 2-year-old had a seizure. The child had a fever of 103°F this morning. The seizure lasted about 2 minutes and the child is now sleepy but responsive. What likely happened?
option_a: The child has epilepsy
option_b: This was likely a febrile seizure which occurs in young children when fever rises rapidly. These are usually brief and benign but frightening for parents. The child should be transported for evaluation to rule out serious causes of fever
option_c: Fevers never cause seizures
rationale: Febrile seizures occur in children 6 months to 5 years with rapid temperature rise. They are usually brief and self-limited. While generally benign the child needs evaluation to determine fever source and rule out meningitis or other serious infections.

question: A restaurant patron begins having difficulty breathing after eating shrimp. She has hives spreading across her chest and her voice sounds hoarse. She says her throat feels like it is closing. She has a known shellfish allergy. What is happening?
option_a: Mild allergic reaction that will resolve
option_b: Anaphylaxis with airway involvement. Throat swelling with difficulty breathing and hoarse voice indicates angioedema that can completely obstruct the airway. This is immediately life-threatening. She needs epinephrine now
option_c: Food poisoning
rationale: Difficulty breathing hoarse voice throat tightness and hives after allergen exposure is anaphylaxis with airway compromise. Angioedema can progress to complete obstruction rapidly. Epinephrine is immediately needed and is life-saving. This is not a minor reaction.

question: The anaphylaxis patient has her prescribed EpiPen. She is too distressed to use it herself. How do you assist with administration?
option_a: Inject into the buttock slowly
option_b: Remove safety cap press the orange tip firmly against her outer thigh until you hear a click and hold for 10 seconds. Can be given through light clothing. Massage the area afterward. Monitor for ongoing symptoms
option_c: Inject into her arm muscle
rationale: EpiPen is administered by firmly pressing into the outer thigh activating the spring-loaded needle. Hold 10 seconds for complete delivery. Can go through light clothing. The thigh has good blood flow for rapid absorption. Massage site to speed distribution.

question: Five minutes after receiving epinephrine the patient's breathing has improved significantly and hives are fading. She wants to go back to dinner and not go to the hospital. What should you advise?
option_a: She is cured and can resume her evening
option_b: Strongly advise transport. Anaphylaxis can recur as epinephrine wears off in 15-20 minutes called biphasic reaction. She needs monitoring and may need additional treatment. Going back to the allergen source is dangerous
option_c: One dose of epinephrine provides permanent protection
option_d: She only needs to take Benadryl now
rationale: Epinephrine effects last only 15-20 minutes. Biphasic anaphylaxis where symptoms return is common and can be severe. All anaphylaxis patients should be transported for monitoring and additional treatment. Returning to the allergen source would be dangerous.

question: A patient was stung by a bee and has localized swelling and redness at the sting site. He has no difficulty breathing no hives beyond the sting area and normal vital signs. Is this anaphylaxis?
option_a: Yes any bee sting reaction is anaphylaxis
option_b: No this is a localized reaction not anaphylaxis. Local swelling redness and pain at the sting site are normal. Anaphylaxis involves systemic symptoms in multiple body systems. Monitor for progression but this appears to be a local reaction
option_c: He needs epinephrine immediately
rationale: Local reactions at the sting site with swelling redness and pain are common and not anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis involves systemic symptoms affecting multiple body systems such as respiratory cardiovascular or GI. Monitor this patient for progression but current presentation is localized.

question: Parents call 911 because their 3-year-old got into grandmother's medication and may have swallowed some pills. The child is currently alert and playful with no symptoms. What should you do?
option_a: No symptoms means no poisoning occurred
option_b: Transport for evaluation. Many toxic ingestions have delayed symptoms. The child appears fine now but could deteriorate. Bring the medication bottle to identify what was taken. Contact Poison Control for guidance on expected symptoms and timing
option_c: Make the child vomit to remove the pills
rationale: Delayed symptom onset is common with many medications. A currently asymptomatic child may become critically ill. Transport for evaluation with the medication bottle for identification. Poison Control can provide guidance on expected toxicity and timing.

question: A family is found unconscious in their home during winter. A kerosene heater is running. You notice the scene is warm but something seems wrong. What should you suspect and do?
option_a: They are just sleeping heavily
option_b: Suspect carbon monoxide poisoning. Remove everyone from the environment immediately to fresh air. Do not enter without proper protection if CO levels may be high. Give high-flow oxygen to all patients. CO is odorless and can be lethal
option_c: The heater is providing too much heat
rationale: Multiple unconscious people in a closed environment with a combustion heat source suggests CO poisoning. CO is odorless and can be lethal. Remove to fresh air give high-flow oxygen and transport. All patients need CO level measurement at hospital.

question: A carbon monoxide poisoning patient has SpO2 reading 99% but is confused and has cherry-red skin. How do you explain the normal oxygen saturation?
option_a: The pulse oximeter proves he is fine
option_b: Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish between hemoglobin bound to oxygen versus carbon monoxide. The SpO2 reads falsely normal or high. Cherry-red skin and altered mental status indicate significant CO poisoning despite the normal reading. Give 100% oxygen
option_c: CO poisoning does not affect SpO2
rationale: Standard pulse oximetry cannot differentiate carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin. CO-poisoned patients have falsely normal SpO2 readings. Clinical signs like altered mental status cherry-red coloring headache and nausea indicate poisoning. Do not rely on SpO2 in suspected CO cases.

question: You find a 22-year-old unresponsive in a bathroom with drug paraphernalia nearby. His respirations are 4 per minute and shallow. Pupils are pinpoint. He has a weak pulse. What is happening and what should you do?
option_a: He is sleeping off his drug use
option_b: This is opioid overdose with life-threatening respiratory depression. Begin assisted ventilations with BVM immediately. Administer naloxone if available per protocol. Pinpoint pupils slow respirations and decreased consciousness are classic opioid overdose
option_c: He is in cardiac arrest start CPR
option_d: Give him stimulants to wake him up
rationale: Pinpoint pupils respiratory depression and decreased consciousness after drug use is classic opioid overdose. He has a pulse so does not need CPR but needs assisted ventilation immediately. Naloxone can reverse the opioid effects but ventilatory support is the priority.

question: After giving naloxone the overdose patient suddenly wakes up becomes agitated and wants to leave. He is verbally abusive and trying to pull out his IV. How should you handle this?
option_a: Restrain him and force him to go to the hospital
option_b: Remain calm and non-confrontational. Explain what happened and that he needs monitoring because naloxone can wear off and he could become unresponsive again. Try to convince him to be transported. If he refuses document capacity and refusal
option_c: Give more naloxone to calm him down
option_d: Leave since he is awake now
rationale: Patients awakened by naloxone often become agitated as they are suddenly in withdrawal. Remain calm and explain the situation including risk of re-sedation when naloxone wears off. Encourage transport. If he refuses and appears to have capacity document the informed refusal thoroughly.

question: Police ask you to evaluate an intoxicated man found wandering the street. He smells strongly of alcohol is confused has slurred speech and unsteady gait. He has a small laceration on his forehead. Can you assume his symptoms are from alcohol?
option_a: Yes alcohol explains all his symptoms
option_b: No. Many conditions mimic intoxication including hypoglycemia head injury stroke and overdose. The forehead laceration suggests trauma and possible head injury. Do not assume alcohol is the only cause. Full assessment and transport for evaluation are warranted
option_c: Intoxicated patients do not need medical care
rationale: Never assume all symptoms are from alcohol intoxication. The forehead laceration raises concern for head injury. Hypoglycemia stroke and drug interactions can mimic or coexist with intoxication. This patient needs full assessment and transport for evaluation.

question: A woman is found on a bridge railing saying she wants to jump. Police have secured the scene. She is crying and says she does not want to live anymore. How should you approach this?
option_a: Tell her she is being selfish and to come down
option_b: Approach calmly and non-threateningly. Speak in a calm reassuring voice. Listen actively and acknowledge her pain without judgment. Do not make promises you cannot keep. Try to establish rapport and encourage her to come to safety. Never rush or grab
option_c: Stay far away and let police handle it
option_d: Tell her things are not that bad
rationale: Suicidal patients need calm non-judgmental approach. Listen actively and acknowledge their pain. Build rapport before trying to convince them to come to safety. Avoid arguing minimizing their feelings or making sudden moves. This requires patience and genuine compassion.

question: An agitated patient is pacing yelling and making threatening statements. He has not become physical yet. Your safety is your priority. How should you position yourself?
option_a: Stand directly in front of him to show you are not afraid
option_b: Position yourself near an exit with a clear escape route. Maintain safe distance. Keep hands visible and posture non-threatening. Speak calmly and avoid arguing. Do not turn your back. Request law enforcement if not already present
option_c: Corner him so he cannot escape
rationale: Provider safety is paramount. Position near exits with escape route. Maintain safe distance and non-threatening posture. Speak calmly and avoid confrontation. Never turn your back on an agitated patient. Request law enforcement for safety. You cannot help if you become a victim.

question: A 35-year-old woman has severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain. She has a fever of 101°F and has been nauseous. The pain is worse with movement. She rates it 8/10. What condition should you suspect?
option_a: Stomach flu
option_b: Possible appendicitis. Right lower quadrant pain with fever and nausea is classic for appendicitis. Worsening with movement suggests peritoneal irritation. She needs surgical evaluation. Do not give food or fluids as surgery may be needed
option_c: Menstrual cramps
rationale: Right lower quadrant pain fever nausea and pain with movement are classic for appendicitis. The appendix is inflamed and may rupture causing peritonitis. This patient needs surgical evaluation. Keep NPO as surgery may be imminent.
